attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-day history of high fevers, severe headache, and muscle pain. Five days ago he returned from a one-week business trip to Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. He reports feeling well during his trip but developed symptoms two days after returning home. He also notes mild pain behind his eyes and some discomfort when moving his eyes. He has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. He stayed in a hotel in the city centre and did not visit rural areas during his trip. His three-year-old daughter did not travel with him as she attends kindergarten and had been unwell with cough and rhinorrhoea for the previous seven days.

On examination, he appears unwell with pulse rate 100/min, blood pressure 110/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 40.1°C. He has a faint, blanching, maculopapular rash on his trunk and limbs which started one day after the onset of fevers. There is no lymphadenopathy, hepatomegaly, or splenomegaly.

Investigations reveal: haemoglobin 145 g/L (130-180), white cell count 2.8 × 10^9/L (4.0-11.0), platelets 95 × 10^9/L (150-400), alanine aminotransferase 78 U/L (5-40), aspartate aminotransferase 65 U/L (5-40).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with a febrile illness after returning from Malaysia, which is endemic for several tropical infections. The clinical picture strongly suggests dengue fever, which is the most common cause of fever in a returned traveller from south-east Asia. Dengue is a virus infection that is spread through the bites of infected mosquitoes (Aedes aegypti or Aedes albopictus). These are the same mosquitoes that can spread Zika and chikungunya viruses.

Key features supporting this diagnosis include the timing of symptom onset (2-7 days after exposure is typical), fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, and a maculopapular rash. Laboratory findings of leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and mildly elevated liver enzymes are characteristic.

Malaria typically presents with cyclical fevers, rigors, and sweats. While it should be considered in any traveller returning from an endemic area with fever, the absence of cyclical symptoms, geographic location and the presence of a rash (uncommon in malaria) make dengue more likely. Additionally, urban Kuala Lumpur has a low risk of malaria transmission compared to rural areas of Malaysia.

Typhoid fever usually has a more gradual onset with step-ladder pattern fevers, relative bradycardia, and abdominal symptoms including constipation or diarrhoea. The rash of typhoid (rose spots) is typically sparse and found on the trunk. The laboratory findings are more consistent with dengue.

Adenovirus infection can cause fever, headache, and malaise, but typically presents with prominent respiratory symptoms (sore throat, cough, rhinorrhoea) or conjunctivitis. While the patient's child has respiratory symptoms suggestive of a viral illness and every febrile returned traveller does not always have a tropical disease, the patient's clinical picture with retro-orbital pain, rash, and characteristic laboratory findings (thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, elevated liver enzymes) is much more consistent with dengue fever than adenovirus infection.

Leptospirosis typically presents with fever, headache, myalgia, and conjunctival suffusion. Exposure history often includes contact with contaminated water or soil, or exposure to rodents. The absence of conjunctival suffusion and lack of exposure history make leptospirosis less likely. Additionally, the pattern of laboratory abnormalities better support dengue.

Dengue fever is endemic in tropical and sub-topical climates, with over 100- 400 million infections per year. The presentation is difficult to differentiate from chikungunya with similar presenting symptoms and endemic regions. The diagnosis is typically confirmed with dengue NS1 antigen testing (positive in the first week) and/or dengue IgM antibodies (which appear later) which is helpful for public health control measures though diagnosis may be clinical. Management is supportive, focusing on adequate hydration and monitoring for warning signs of severe dengue, such as persistent vomiting, abdominal pain, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, or liver enlargement.
learningPoints: Dengue fever should be considered in patients with fever, headache, and myalgia returning from endemic areas like Malaysia.
Thrombocytopenia, leukopenia, and elevated liver enzymes are common laboratory findings in dengue fever.
The clinical presentation of dengue includes fever, headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, and a maculopapular rash.
references: World Health Organisation. Dengue and Severe Dengue [Internet]. World Health Organization. 2024. Available from: https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/dengue-and-severe-dengue

Liao W, Runnegar N. Rash in a returned traveller from Bali. Australian Journal of General Practice. 2019: 48 (6).  Available at: https://www1.racgp.org.au/getattachment/7928d043-411d-4053-81a8-b3da664fccdc/Rash-in-a-returned-traveller.aspx
